title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: psvr R package implementing four SVR models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. targets forecasting contexts targets yk > 0 strictly positive relative accuracy matters absolute accuracy. Source paper: Unified Family Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression Models Symmetric Kernel Extensions (MDPI Mathematics, 2026). Full derivations paper.tex repository root.

what: Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 1 / Appendix A.1)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: Dual QP (max form): βk = αk - αk*, |βk| = αk + αk*. KKT: ω = Σk βk φ(xk), Σk βk = 0, 0 ≤ αk, αk* ≤ 100C/yk. Prediction: f(x) = Σk βk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Key files: R/mape_svr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + C Σk (ξk + ξk*) s.t. (100/yk)(yk - ωᵀφ(xk) - b) ≤ ε + ξk      (100/yk)(ωᵀφ(xk) + b - yk) ≤ ε + ξk*      ξk, ξk* ≥ 0 max  -½ Σk,l βk βl K(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (sample-dependent box; yk small → tight bound)

what: Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 2 / Appendix A.2)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 1, augmented symmetry constraint: ωᵀφ(xk) = · ωᵀφ(-xk) Dual QP (max form): Ks(xi,xj) = K(xi,xj) + ·K(xi,-xj). factor ¼ (vs ½ Model 1) arises symmetrization representer theorem (Appendix .2): ωᵀφ(xk) = ½ Σl βl Ks(xl, xk), quadratic term picks extra ½. Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk βk Ks(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Build Ks = Ω + ·Ω* inline (½), pass P = ¼·Ks QP quadratic matrix — ¼ factor absorbed P directly. use sym_kernel_matrix() ; helper returns ½(Ω + ·Ω*) = Ωs, correct Model 4. Key files: R/mape_sym_svr.R.
code: max  -¼ Σk,l βk βl Ks(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (unchanged from Model 1)

what: Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 3 / Appendix A.3)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: KKT stationarity: - ω = Σk αk φ(xk) - Σk αk = 0 - ek = (yk²/Γ) αk Linear system (solve directly): YΓ = diag(y1²/Γ, …, yN²/Γ) — added diagonal Ω . Prediction: f(x) = Σk αk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: base::solve() (N+1)×(N+1) augmented matrix. Key files: R/rmspe_lssvr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + (Γ/2) Σk ek²/yk² s.t. yk = ωᵀφ(xk) + b + ek [ 0    1ᵀ      ] [b]   [0] [ 1    Ω + YΓ  ] [α] = [y]

what: Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 4 / Appendix A.4)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 3, augmented symmetry constraint. Linear system: Ωs = ½(Ω + aΩ*), Ω*kl = K(xk, -xl). Symmetric representer: ωᵀφ(xk) = Σl (Ωs)kl αl Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk αk Ks(xk, x) + b equivalently: f(x) = Σk αk [K(xk,x) + ·K(xk,-x)] / 2 + b Implementation: base::solve() Ωs replacing Ω. Key files: R/rmspe_sym_lssvr.R.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Required Models 2 4: - K(-xi, xj) = K(xi, -xj) - K(-xi, -xj) = K(xi, xj) RBF kernel K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖²/2σ²) satisfies conditions: - K(-xi, -xj) = exp(-‖-xi-(-xj)‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,xj) ✓ - K(-xi, xj) = exp(-‖-xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi+xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,-xj) ✓ Polynomial kernels even degree also satisfy Assumption 3.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: QP solver: osqp package (Models 1 & 2). Supports sparse matrices, commercial license. Linear solver: base::solve() (Models 3 & 4). Dense (N+1)×(N+1) system, extra dep. Target validation: fit() functions call stopifnot((y > 0)). Kernel interface: make_kernel(type, ...) returns closure function(xi, xj) type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. Shared across four models. YΓ storage: stored vector y^2 / gamma; added diagonal via diag(Omega) <- diag(Omega) + y_gamma — never materializes full N×N diagonal matrix. "psvr_fit" — single class returned [psvr()] (post-F1 unified API). Legacy classes "psvr_mape", "psvr_mape_sym", "psvr_rmspe", "psvr_rmspe_sym" still returned deprecated wrappers R/deprecated.R; predict/print/coef methods still registered. S3 methods: predict(), print(), coef(), summary() psvr_fit; predict() / print() / coef() legacy class. Documentation: roxygen2 @param, @return, @examples. Style: tidyverse / base R style: snake_case, <- assignment. License: MIT.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Pass 1 runs plain power iteration Ωs returns Rayleigh quotient lambda_max_hat. Pass 2 runs power iteration rho * - Ωs rho = |lambda_max_hat| (spectral radius) returns Rayleigh lambda_min_hat = v^T Ωs v converged v. passes O(N²); total cost 2 * T_pi matvecs.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression

Pass 2 shift uses |Pass 1 Rayleigh|, Pass 1’s signed Rayleigh, handle λ_min-dominant case |λ_min| > λ_max Pass 1 converges v_min negative Rayleigh; using abs(...) ensures rho * - Ωs PSD, Pass 2 reliably finds v_min(Ωs) regardless side spectrum dominated Pass 1. deviation documented inline R/kernel-spectral.R flagged paper-side erratum (smo-v3.tex line 3467) F8.

what: Why the shifted branch is dormant in production
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: three Mercer kernels supplied make_kernel() ("rbf", "linear", "polynomial"), Ωs = ½(Ω + ·Ω*) always PSD Aronszajn’s closure (shift-invariant kernels) Schur’s product theorem (polynomial kernels collapse K - K* odd / even degrees non-negative linear combinations valid Mercer kernels). shifted branch therefore defensive guard activates non-Mercer kernels (e.g., tanh / sigmoid parameter ranges) supplied via custom kernel closure — use case currently documented tested. Production fits supplied make_kernel() types always take -shift branch remain bit-identical F2.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Theorem 2() guarantees spectrally-shifted matrix Omega_use satisfies Omega_use ⪰ delta_stab * limit T_pi → ∞. finite T_pi, estimate λ_min residual bias bleeds Omega_use; resulting matrix “almost PSD” may strictly clear delta_stab floor. kernel matrices well-separated spectra (typical case Mercer kernels decaying eigenvalues), T_pi = 5 suffices ~10⁻⁶ accuracy lambda_min_hat, Omega_use cleanly clears floor. pathological cases clustered spectra (e.g., Wigner-random matrices, eigenvalues fill interval [-rho, rho] densely per semicircle law), Pass 2’s convergence rate approximately 1, reaching strict floor requires T_pi ~ 200 iterations. Practically, even T_pi = 20 reduces indefiniteness ~1000× pathological cases, sufficient SMO solver handle via existing 0.5e-6 diagonal jitter convergence safeguards. Callers can override default via .adaptive_spectral_shift(Omega_s, T_pi = 200). Note: three Mercer kernels supported make_kernel() (RBF, linear, polynomial), Ωs always PSD (see “shifted branch dormant” ), shifted branch unreachable production limitations user-visible.

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), misaligned scale-free accuracy criteria standard forecasting. error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 large 2. psvr implements four SVR variants derived percentage-error loss functions (Benavides-Herrera et al., 2026), accessed unified psvr() entry point: sym = +1L enforces even symmetry f(x) = f(-x); sym = -1L enforces odd symmetry. Use symmetric variants kernels satisfy Assumption 3 paper (RBF even-degree polynomial kernels ). four legacy fitters (mape_svr(), mape_sym_svr(), rmspe_lssvr(), rmspe_sym_lssvr()) remain available soft-deprecated wrappers removed future release. models require strictly positive targets (y > 0), condition percentage residuals well-defined.

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: # CRAN (once released) install.packages("psvr")  # Development version from GitHub remotes::install_github("pbenavidesh/psvr")

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: reproduce experiments reported Tables 1–2 paper using Boston Housing dataset (Harrison & Rubinfeld, 1978). 506 observations strictly positive median home values (MEDV, thousands USD), making MAPE well-defined throughout.
code: library(psvr) library(ggplot2)  # Boston Housing is available in the MASS package data("Boston", package = "MASS")  # Target: median home value (all > 0) y_all <- Boston$medv X_raw <- as.matrix(Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")])  stopifnot(all(y_all > 0)) cat("N =", nrow(X_raw), "  p =", ncol(X_raw),     "  y range: [", round(min(y_all), 1), ",", round(max(y_all), 1), "]\n") #> N = 506   p = 13   y range: [ 5 , 50 ]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Features standardised using training-set statistics RBF kernel operates comparable scale across 13 predictors.
code: set.seed(42) n      <- nrow(X_raw) tr_idx <- sample(n, floor(0.7 * n))  X_raw_tr <- X_raw[tr_idx, ];  y_tr <- y_all[tr_idx] X_raw_te <- X_raw[-tr_idx, ]; y_te <- y_all[-tr_idx]  # Standardise: centre and scale by training mean/sd col_mean <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) col_sd   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd) X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: mape  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100 rmspe <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 r2    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: lm_df_tr <- as.data.frame(X_tr) lm_df_te <- as.data.frame(X_te) lm_fit   <- lm(y_tr ~ ., data = lm_df_tr) lm_pred  <- predict(lm_fit, newdata = lm_df_te)  cat(sprintf("Linear regression — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, lm_pred), rmspe(y_te, lm_pred), r2(y_te, lm_pred))) #> Linear regression — MAPE: 16.29%  RMSPE: 22.48%  R²: 0.7730

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: LS-SVR formulation replaces QP linear system using quadratic penalty percentage residuals. dual reduces : [0𝟏⊤𝟏Ω+YΓ][b𝛂]=[0𝐲] \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \mathbf{1}^\top \\ \mathbf{1} & \Omega + Y_\Gamma \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b \\ \boldsymbol{\alpha} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \mathbf{y} \end{bmatrix} YΓ=diag⁡(y12/Γ,…,yN2/Γ)Y_\Gamma = \operatorname{diag}(y_1^2/\Gamma, \ldots, y_N^2/\Gamma).
code: # make_kernel() returns a closure K(xi, xj) = exp(-||xi - xj||^2 / (2 sigma^2)) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # gamma = 5000: regularisation; larger gamma -> smaller Y_Gamma diagonal -> tighter fit fit_ls <- psvr(X_tr, y_tr, loss = "rmspe", kernel = K, gamma = 5000) pred_ls <- predict(fit_ls, X_te)  cat(sprintf("LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, pred_ls), rmspe(y_te, pred_ls), r2(y_te, pred_ls))) #> LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: 15.46%  RMSPE: 27.21%  R²: 0.7277 print(fit_ls) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_fit] #>  #>   Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 1) #>   Gamma:           5000 #>   Training obs.:   354 cf_ls <- coef(fit_ls) # alpha:        N dual variables; weight each training point's kernel #               contribution in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b #               (all N points, no sparsity) # b:            bias / intercept term # support_data: all N training inputs stored for prediction cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf_ls$b, min(cf_ls$alpha), max(cf_ls$alpha))) #> b = 22.5268  |  alpha range: [-59.7517, 43.7321]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: ε-SVR formulation optimises QP sample-dependent box constraints |βk|≤100C/yk|\beta_k| \le 100C/y_k: tighter bounds small targets, concentrating model capacity low-magnitude observations.
code:
# C = 10: per-sample box bound |beta_k| <= 100*C/y_k; eps = 1: tube width (% of y_k) fit_ep <- psvr(X_tr, y_tr, loss = "mape", kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 1) pred_ep <- predict(fit_ep, X_te)  cat(sprintf("ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f
",             mape(y_te, pred_ep), rmspe(y_te, pred_ep), r2(y_te, pred_ep))) #> ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: 15.76%  RMSPE: 27.54%  R²: 0.7617 cat(sprintf("Support vectors: %d / %d
", fit_ep$n_sv, fit_ep$n_train)) #> Support vectors: 327 / 354 print(fit_ep) #>  #> epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss  [psvr_fit] #>  #>   Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 1) #>   C:               10 #>   eps:             1 #>   Training obs.:   354 #>   Support vectors: 327 (92.4%) cf_ep <- coef(fit_ep) # alpha:        beta_k = alpha_k - alpha_k* for each support vector; #               non-zero only for training points outside the #               percentage-error ε-tube (sparse) # b:            bias / intercept term # support_data: training rows corresponding to support vectors only cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_ep$b, min(cf_ep$alpha), max(cf_ep$alpha))) #> b = 23.1087  |  alpha range: [-110.6876, 82.6446]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Test-set performance Boston Housing (70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run). psvr models improve linear baseline respective percentage-error objectives. LS-SVR formulation (Model 3) minimises RMSPE directly achieves lowest squared-percentage error; ε-SVR formulation (Model 1) targets MAPE sample-dependent box constraints dual variables. results consistent Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), SVR-MAPE reduces test-set MAPE 15.23% (classical ε-SVR) around 10% relative linear baseline 17.85%.
code: results <- data.frame(   Model = c("Linear regression", "LS-SVR RMSPE (Model 3)",             "\u03b5-SVR MAPE (Model 1)"),   MAPE  = c(mape(y_te,  lm_pred),             mape(y_te,  pred_ls),             mape(y_te,  pred_ep)),   RMSPE = c(rmspe(y_te, lm_pred),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ls),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ep)),   R2    = c(r2(y_te,    lm_pred),             r2(y_te,    pred_ls),             r2(y_te,    pred_ep)) ) results[, 2:4] <- round(results[, 2:4], 2) knitr::kable(results, col.names = c("Model", "MAPE (%)", "RMSPE (%)", "R²"),              align = "lrrr",              caption = paste("Test-set performance on Boston Housing",                              "(70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run)."))

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: vignette verifies R implementations psvr reproduce numerical results reported Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), originally computed Python using cvxopt (Models 1 & 2) numpy.linalg.lstsq (Models 3 & 4). verification strategy : Use dataset, split seed, hyperparameters Python experiments. Report four metrics: MAPE, RMSPE, R², MSE test set. Compare R outputs paper reference values. Small numerical differences expected acceptable: Python implementation uses cvxopt QP (Models 1 & 2) pseudoinverse via numpy.linalg.lstsq linear system (Models 3 & 4), whereas psvr uses osqp base::solve(), respectively. approaches solve mathematical problem use different numerical algorithms different stopping tolerances, can produce discrepancies order 0.01–0.1 percentage points reported metrics.

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
code: library(psvr) library(ggplot2)  # Boston Housing — all 506 observations, MEDV strictly positive data("Boston", package = "MASS")  y_all <- Boston$medv X_raw <- as.matrix(Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")])  stopifnot(all(y_all > 0)) cat("N =", nrow(X_raw), " p =", ncol(X_raw),     " y range: [", round(min(y_all), 1), ",", round(max(y_all), 1), "]\n") #> N = 506  p = 13  y range: [ 5 , 50 ]

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Python experiments used train_test_split(..., test_size=0.30, random_state=4). replicate set.seed(4) stratified-equivalent random draw.
code: set.seed(4) n      <- nrow(X_raw) tr_idx <- sample(n, floor(0.7 * n))  X_raw_tr <- X_raw[tr_idx, ];  y_tr <- y_all[tr_idx] X_raw_te <- X_raw[-tr_idx, ]; y_te <- y_all[-tr_idx]  cat("Training:", nrow(X_raw_tr), " Test:", nrow(X_raw_te), "\n") #> Training: 354  Test: 152

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Features centred scaled using training-set mean standard deviation, mimicking StandardScaler().fit(X_train).transform(...).
code: col_mean <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) col_sd   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd)  X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd)

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
code: mape_fn  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100 rmspe_fn <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 r2_fn    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2) mse_fn   <- function(y, yhat) mean((y - yhat)^2)

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Hyperparameters Table 1 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026): Python RBF kernel uses gamma_py convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp⁡(−γpy∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma_{\text{py}} \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2), equivalent psvr RBF convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp⁡(−∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2/2σ2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2 / 2\sigma^2) σ=1/(2γpy)\sigma = \sqrt{1 / (2\,\gamma_{\text{py}})}.
code:
sigma_m3 <- sqrt(1 / (2 * 0.050967)) cat("Model 3 — sigma:", round(sigma_m3, 6), "
") #> Model 3 — sigma: 3.132135  K3  <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = sigma_m3) fit_m3 <- psvr(X_tr, y_tr, loss = "rmspe", kernel = K3, gamma = 16962.540770) pred_m3 <- predict(fit_m3, X_te)  m3_mape  <- mape_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_rmspe <- rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_r2    <- r2_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_mse   <- mse_fn(y_te, pred_m3)  cat(sprintf(   "Model 3 (R) — MAPE: %.4f%%  RMSPE: %.4f%%  R²: %.6f  MSE: %.6f
",   m3_mape, m3_rmspe, m3_r2, m3_mse )) #> Model 3 (R) — MAPE: 11.0495%  RMSPE: 18.7205%  R²: 0.819584  MSE: 14.127632 print(fit_m3) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_fit] #>  #>   Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 3.13213) #>   Gamma:           16962.5 #>   Training obs.:   354 cf_m3 <- coef(fit_m3) # alpha:        N dual variables; weight each training point in #               f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b # b:            bias / intercept term # support_data: all N training inputs (LS-SVR — no sparsity, every point #               contributes) cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_m3$b, min(cf_m3$alpha), max(cf_m3$alpha))) #> b = 25.1360  |  alpha range: [-774.4447, 307.9511]

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Hyperparameters Table 2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026):
code:
sigma_m1 <- sqrt(1 / (2 * 0.032920)) cat("Model 1 — sigma:", round(sigma_m1, 6), "
") #> Model 1 — sigma: 3.897221  K1  <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = sigma_m1) fit_m1 <- psvr(X_tr, y_tr, loss = "mape", kernel = K1,                C = 15.631280, eps = 5.804601) pred_m1 <- predict(fit_m1, X_te)  m1_mape  <- mape_fn(y_te, pred_m1) m1_rmspe <- rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m1) m1_r2    <- r2_fn(y_te, pred_m1) m1_mse   <- mse_fn(y_te, pred_m1)  cat(sprintf(   "Model 1 (R) — MAPE: %.4f%%  RMSPE: %.4f%%  R²: %.6f  MSE: %.6f
",   m1_mape, m1_rmspe, m1_r2, m1_mse )) #> Model 1 (R) — MAPE: 10.6892%  RMSPE: 18.5526%  R²: 0.810705  MSE: 14.822893 cat(sprintf("Support vectors: %d / %d training points
",             fit_m1$n_sv, fit_m1$n_train)) #> Support vectors: 214 / 354 training points print(fit_m1) #>  #> epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss  [psvr_fit] #>  #>   Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 3.89722) #>   C:               15.6313 #>   eps:             5.8046 #>   Training obs.:   354 #>   Support vectors: 214 (60.5%) cf_m1 <- coef(fit_m1) # alpha:        beta_k = alpha_k - alpha_k* for each support vector; #               non-zero for points outside the percentage-error ε-tube #               (sparse) # b:            bias / intercept term # support_data: training rows for support vectors only cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_m1$b, min(cf_m1$alpha), max(cf_m1$alpha))) #> b = 25.4658  |  alpha range: [-248.1156, 163.4649]

text: table juxtaposes R results computed Python reference values reported Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026). metrics directly comparable two implementations use different train/test splits: set.seed(4); sample() R seeds R’s Mersenne Twister, random_state=4 sklearn seeds numpy’s Mersenne Twister. two generators produce different random sequences integer seed, 152 test observations differ two runs. mathematical equivalence implementations verified KKT unit tests tests/testthat/, matching held-metrics. Test-set metrics — Boston Housing, 70/30 split. R rows: set.seed(4) R’s RNG. Python rows: random_state=4 numpy’s RNG (different test set). title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: observed gaps (e.g. R² 0.82 vs 0.90 Model 3) evidence implementation bug. Numerical investigation confirms three things: Non-equivalent splits dominant cause. set.seed(4) seeds R’s Mersenne Twister; random_state=4 seeds numpy’s Mersenne Twister. independent generators happen share API convention seed integer produce entirely different random sequences. 152 test observations selected different, metrics computed different data. linear solver factor. Replacing base::solve() MASS::ginv() (pseudoinverse via SVD, matching Python’s numpy.linalg.lstsq) changes predictions 3 × 10⁻¹² dataset. augmented system condition number κ ≈ 2 100, LU decomposition SVD agree machine precision. QP solver minor factor. osqp (ADMM) cvxopt (interior-point) solve QP different tolerances, producing slightly different dual variables. accounts 0.1–0.2 percentage points MAPE, multi-point gaps seen . correct way verify mathematical equivalence KKT conditions: unit tests tests/testthat/ check fitted models satisfy KKT stationarity, complementary slackness, feasibility conditions within solver tolerance, independently particular split.

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: Standard regression models — including classical SVR — minimise absolute-error losses MSE MAE. losses scale-dependent: error 5 units treated identically whether target 10 10,000. many practical settings, however, matters relative error. forecast 5% 200 MPa concrete specimen equivalent practical terms one 5% 20 MPa specimen — even though absolute errors differ factor ten. psvr package provides four SVR variants optimise percentage-error losses directly: Model 1 (psvr_mape_rbf): ε\varepsilon-SVR MAPE loss Model 2 (psvr_mape_sym_rbf): symmetric-kernel extension Model 1 Model 3 (psvr_rmspe_rbf): LS-SVR RMSPE loss Model 4 (psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf): symmetric-kernel extension Model 3 article asks concrete question: dataset relative accuracy matters, models optimise percentage error directly outperform models optimise absolute error? Note psvr universal replacement standard SVR. models require strictly positive targets best suited settings : () target spans wide range scales, (b) domain specifications stated percentage terms, (c) models trained different units datasets need compared fairly.

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
code: library(tidyverse) library(tidymodels) library(psvr) library(modeldata) library(future)  tidymodels_prefer() theme_set(theme_bw())

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: concrete dataset (Yeh, 1998) contains 1,030 observations concrete mixture compositions resulting compressive strength curing. target — compressive strength MPa — strictly positive spans roughly 2.3 82.6 MPa, range 35-fold. wide scale variation makes natural candidate percentage-error evaluation.  Compressive strength spans wide range, motivating relative error metrics. distribution right-skewed long upper tail. dataset? 35-fold range compressive strength means absolute-error loss weights high-strength specimens far heavily low-strength ones. model optimised MSE can achieve low aggregate error fitting high-strength region well relatively inaccurate low-strength concrete — may structurally critical regime.

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: four models use RBF kernel. symmetry parameter = 1 (even symmetry) fixed engine argument — encodes assumption regression function satisfies f(𝐱)=f(−𝐱)f(\mathbf{x}) = f(-\mathbf{x}), reasonable centering via step_normalize. Hyperparameter search ranges psvr package supplies custom dials parameters appropriate defaults percentage-error models: svm_margin → margin_percentage(): default range [1, 20] percentage units — manual override needed. cost → cost_psvr(): default range [−2, 10] log₂ scale (~0.25 1,024) — manual override needed. rbf_sigma → rbf_sigma_psvr(): default range [−3, 1] log₁₀ scale. range adjusted using median-distance heuristic via sigma_heuristic() — see code .
code: spec_lm  <- linear_reg() |>   set_engine("lm")  spec_svm <- svm_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune()) |>   set_engine("kernlab") |>   set_mode("regression")  spec_rf  <- rand_forest(mtry = tune(), trees = 500, min_n = tune()) |>   set_engine("ranger") |>   set_mode("regression")  spec_xgb <- boost_tree(   trees     = 500,   tree_depth = tune(),   learn_rate = tune(),   loss_reduction = tune() ) |>   set_engine("xgboost") |>   set_mode("regression") spec_m1 <- psvr_mape_rbf(   cost       = tune(),   svm_margin = tune(),   rbf_sigma  = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m2 <- psvr_mape_sym_rbf(   cost       = tune(),   svm_margin = tune(),   rbf_sigma  = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m3 <- psvr_rmspe_rbf(   cost      = tune(),   rbf_sigma = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m4 <- psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(   cost      = tune(),   rbf_sigma = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr") # Compute data-driven rbf_sigma parameter from the normalised training predictors train_baked      <- rec_base |> prep() |> bake(new_data = train) rbf_sigma_custom <- rbf_sigma_psvr_data(   train_baked |> select(-compressive_strength) )  r <- dials::range_get(rbf_sigma_custom, original = FALSE) cat(sprintf("rbf_sigma range: [%.3f, %.3f]\n", r$lower, r$upper)) rbf_sigma range: [-0.433, 1.567]

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: four models use RBF kernel. symmetry parameter = 1 (even symmetry) fixed engine argument — encodes assumption regression function satisfies f(𝐱)=f(−𝐱)f(\mathbf{x}) = f(-\mathbf{x}), reasonable centering via step_normalize. Hyperparameter search ranges psvr package supplies custom dials parameters appropriate defaults percentage-error models: svm_margin → margin_percentage(): default range [1, 20] percentage units — manual override needed. cost → cost_psvr(): default range [−2, 10] log₂ scale (~0.25 1,024) — manual override needed. rbf_sigma → rbf_sigma_psvr(): default range [−3, 1] log₁₀ scale. range adjusted using median-distance heuristic via sigma_heuristic() — see code .
code: spec_m1 <- psvr_mape_rbf(   cost       = tune(),   svm_margin = tune(),   rbf_sigma  = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m2 <- psvr_mape_sym_rbf(   cost       = tune(),   svm_margin = tune(),   rbf_sigma  = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m3 <- psvr_rmspe_rbf(   cost      = tune(),   rbf_sigma = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m4 <- psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(   cost      = tune(),   rbf_sigma = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr") # Compute data-driven rbf_sigma parameter from the normalised training predictors train_baked      <- rec_base |> prep() |> bake(new_data = train) rbf_sigma_custom <- rbf_sigma_psvr_data(   train_baked |> select(-compressive_strength) )  r <- dials::range_get(rbf_sigma_custom, original = FALSE) cat(sprintf("rbf_sigma range: [%.3f, %.3f]\n", r$lower, r$upper)) rbf_sigma range: [-0.433, 1.567]

bars show ±1.96 SE"   ) rank_res |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   select(rank, wflow_id, mean, std_err, n) |>   rename(     Rank     = rank,     Workflow = wflow_id,     MAPE     = mean,     SE       = std_err,     Folds    = n   ) |>   mutate(across(c(MAPE, SE), \(x) round(x, 2))) |>   knitr::kable(caption = "Cross-validated MAPE — best configuration per workflow") best_mape <- rank_results(tune_res, rank_metric = "mape", select_best = TRUE) |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   select(wflow_id, mape = mean)  best_rmse <- rank_results(tune_res, rank_metric = "rmse", select_best = TRUE) |>   filter(.metric == "rmse") |>   select(wflow_id, rmse = mean)  tradeoff <- left_join(best_mape, best_rmse, by = "wflow_id") |>   mutate(     family = case_when(       str_detect(wflow_id, "m1|m2|m3|m4") ~ "psvr",       str_detect(wflow_id, "svm")          ~ "SVM (kernlab)",       str_detect(wflow_id, "rf")           ~ "Random Forest",       str_detect(wflow_id, "xgb")          ~ "XGBoost",       TRUE                                  ~ "Linear"     ),     label = str_remove(wflow_id, "^base_")   )  tradeoff |>   ggplot(aes(x = rmse, y = mape, colour = family, label = label)) +   geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.85) +   ggrepel::geom_text_repel(size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +   scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     x        = "Cross-validated RMSE (MPa)",     y        = "Cross-validated MAPE (%)",     colour   = "Model family",     title    = "MAPE vs. RMSE trade-off",     subtitle = "Each point = best hyperparameter config for that workflow"   )

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: psvr implements four support vector regression models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), scale-dependent: error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 critical 2. psvr addresses optimising MAPE RMSPE directly, making well-suited forecasting tasks targets strictly positive relative accuracy matters. models require strictly positive targets (y > 0).

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
code: # Development version from GitHub remotes::install_github("pbenavidesh/psvr")

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: full worked example Boston Housing dataset — including 70/30 train–test split, feature standardisation, comparison linear baseline — see vignette("getting-started", package = "psvr").
code:
library(psvr)  # Synthetic dataset: even function (f(-x) = f(x)), targets strictly positive set.seed(42) n  <- 100 X  <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) y  <- 2 + X[, 1]^2 + 0.5 * X[, 2]^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1)  tr <- 1:70;  te <- 71:100 X_tr <- X[tr, ];  y_tr <- y[tr] X_te <- X[te, ];  y_te <- y[te]  # Standardise features using training-set statistics (important for RBF) col_mean <- colMeans(X_tr);  col_sd <- apply(X_tr, 2, sd) X_tr_s   <- scale(X_tr, col_mean, col_sd) X_te_s   <- scale(X_te, col_mean, col_sd)  K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE fit1  <- mape_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5) pred1 <- predict(fit1, X_te_s)  # Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit2  <- mape_sym_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5, a = 1) pred2 <- predict(fit2, X_te_s)  # Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE fit3  <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100) pred3 <- predict(fit3, X_te_s)  # Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit4  <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100, a = 1) pred4 <- predict(fit4, X_te_s)  mape <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100  cat(sprintf("Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred1))) cat(sprintf("Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred2))) cat(sprintf("Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred3))) cat(sprintf("Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred4))) #> Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = 3.59% #> Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = 6.04% #> Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = 3.76% #> Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = 5.79%

Default 4 (≈16× headroom).  Negative values accepted warning, since resulting upper bound falls var(y) * n heuristic unlikely useful.

title: Data-driven cost range for LS-SVR psvr models — cost_psvr_ls_data
text: default width_log2 = 4, upper bound covers typical optimum within ~2 orders magnitude benchmark datasets.  Boston Housing (var(medv) ≈ 84.6, N_train = 404 80/20 split), gives upper bound 2^19.06 ≈ 5.4 × 10⁵, two decades published optimum Γ ≈ 1.7 × 10⁴ — comfortable headroom Bayesian optimisation without boundary trapping.  static cost_psvr() range [-2, 10] (.e. Γ ≤ 1024) underestimates decade. Use function m3 (LS-SVR) m4 (symmetric LS-SVR) workflows.  Stick cost_psvr() m1/m2 (ε-SVR), cost maps C typical optima lie [10, 100].

title: Adaptive spectral regularization (internal, F3) — .adaptive_spectral_shift
text: Implements Theorem 2 arXiv:2605.01446 v3 corrected eigenvalue estimator. Adds mu * shift Omega_s smallest eigenvalue numerically negative, SMO Hessian provably PSD (>= delta_stab * ). Returns matrix untouched already PSD.

title: Adaptive spectral regularization (internal, F3) — .adaptive_spectral_shift
code: .adaptive_spectral_shift(Omega_s, T_pi = 5L, delta_stab = 1e-08)

title: Adaptive spectral regularization (internal, F3) — .adaptive_spectral_shift
text: Omega_s Symmetrized kernel matrix Omega_s = 1/2 (Omega + * Omega^*), already caller-applied jitter. T_pi Power-iteration steps per pass (default 5L). delta_stab Numerical PSD floor (default 1e-8).

title: Adaptive spectral regularization (internal, F3) — .adaptive_spectral_shift
text: list components: Omega_use Matrix pass SMO/QP solver. mu Numeric scalar; shift applied (0 shift). lambda_min_hat Numeric scalar; Rayleigh-quotient estimate lambda_min(Omega_s) Pass 2. lambda_max_hat Numeric scalar; Pass 1's Rayleigh quotient. PSD lambda_max-dominant matrices (typical case Mercer kernels package), equals lambda_max(Omega_s). lambda_min-dominant matrices (|lambda_min| > lambda_max — pathological case reachable make_kernel()-supplied kernels), equals lambda_min(Omega_s). branch decision either case made correctly via Pass 2 estimate lambda_min_hat. branch_taken Character "no_shift" "shifted". n_power_iterations Integer vector length 2; iterations executed Pass 1 Pass 2.

title: Adaptive spectral regularization (internal, F3) — .adaptive_spectral_shift
text: Algorithm 2 line 6 paper, literally written (v <- -Omega_s * v / ||Omega_s * v||), estimates -lambda_max(Omega_s) rather lambda_min(Omega_s): power iteration -Omega_s converges eigenvector largest |eigenvalue|, v_max(Omega_s) whenever |lambda_max| > |lambda_min| (typical case Mercer-PSD near-PSD matrices). implementation uses standard two-pass shifted power iteration: Pass 1 estimates lambda_max via power iteration Omega_s; Pass 2 estimates lambda_min via power iteration rho * - Omega_s (whose dominant eigenvector v_min(Omega_s)). passes O(N^2); total cost 2 * T_pi matvecs. Pass 2 shift uses spectral radius rho = |Pass 1 Rayleigh|, Pass 1's signed Rayleigh. handles lambda_min-dominant pathological case (|lambda_min| > lambda_max), plain power iteration Omega_s Pass 1 converges v_min gives negative Rayleigh; using abs(Pass 1 Rayleigh) ensures shifted operator rho * - Omega_s PSD, Pass 2 reliably finds v_min(Omega_s) regardless side spectrum dominated Pass 1.

title: Adaptive spectral regularization (internal, F3) — .adaptive_spectral_shift

Cached construction. get_entry(p, q) Returns scalar Omega[p, q]. get_matvec(v) Returns matrix-vector product .numeric(Omega %*% v) (length-N). title: Build a kernel-matrix accessor (internal) — .make_kernel_accessor

Symmetry assumption. wrapped matrix assumed symmetric. \(\Omega\) (kernel matrix symmetric K) \(\Omega_s = \tfrac{1}{2}(\Omega + \,\Omega^*)\) (used symmetric models K satisfies Assumption 3) symmetric throughout package, callers may use get_column(p)[k] place row read Omega[p, k]. SMO solver relies WSS3 step.

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: three supported kernels : RBF: K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖² / (2 * sigma²)) Linear: K(xi, xj) = xi · xj Polynomial: K(xi, xj) = (xi · xj + coef0)^degree RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 paper (kernel symmetry), making compatible symmetric models. linear kernel odd-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 used mape_sym_svr() rmspe_sym_lssvr().

what: psvr: Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression — psvr-package
title: psvr: Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression — psvr-package
text: Implements four support vector regression models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions: epsilon-SVR MAPE, symmetric kernel extension, LS-SVR RMSPE, symmetric counterpart. models require strictly positive targets. epsilon-SVR models solved via built-SMO algorithm (osqp available optional alternative backend) LS-SVR models via linear system (base R). See Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026, review MDPI Mathematics) mathematical derivations.

title: psvr: Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression — psvr-package
text: Maintainer: Pablo Benavides-Herrera pbenavides@iteso.mx (ORCID)

what: Fit a percentage-error SVR / LS-SVR model — psvr
title: Fit a percentage-error SVR / LS-SVR model — psvr
text: Unified entry point four model families psvr package: MAPE epsilon-SVR (Model 1), symmetric MAPE epsilon-SVR (Model 2), RMSPE LS-SVR (Model 3), symmetric RMSPE LS-SVR (Model 4). Selection driven loss ("mape" "rmspe") sym (NULL, +1L, -1L). four legacy public fitters (mape_svr(), mape_sym_svr(), rmspe_lssvr(), rmspe_sym_lssvr()) remain available slated deprecation.

title: Fit a percentage-error SVR / LS-SVR model — psvr
code: psvr(   X,   y,   loss = c("mape", "rmspe"),   sym = NULL,   kernel,   C = NULL,   eps = NULL,   gamma = NULL,   solver = c("smo", "osqp"),   tol = 1e-05,   precondition = "auto",   alpha_init = NULL,   alpha_star_init = NULL,   reg = NULL,   ... )

title: Fit a percentage-error SVR / LS-SVR model — psvr
text: X Numeric matrix training inputs, one observation per row (N × p). y Numeric vector training targets (length N). Must satisfy y > 0. loss One "mape" (epsilon-SVR MAPE loss) "rmspe" (LS-SVR RMSPE loss). sym Symmetry knob. NULL (default) fits non-symmetric model; +1L enforces even symmetry f(x) = f(-x); -1L enforces odd symmetry f(x) = -f(-x). symmetric-kernel assumption (Assumption 3 paper) must hold; see make_kernel(). kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). C Regularization parameter C > 0 (loss = "mape" ). eps Insensitivity tube half-width eps >= 0 percentage units (loss = "mape" ). gamma Regularization parameter Γ > 0 (loss = "rmspe" ). solver Backend dual QP, "smo" (default) "osqp" (loss = "mape" ). tol Solver zero-threshold (loss = "mape" ). precondition One "auto" (default), "always", "never", positive numeric threshold; controls Remark-17 symmetric rescaling (loss = "rmspe" ). See rmspe_lssvr() semantics. alpha_init, alpha_star_init, reg Reserved future phases (warm start, extended Lagrangian). Must NULL F1. ... Currently unused; reserved future extension.

title: Fit a percentage-error SVR / LS-SVR model — psvr
text: object class "psvr_fit", list components: loss "mape" "rmspe". sym NULL, +1L, -1L. kernel kernel closure used. alpha Dual coefficients. loss = "mape" holds α − α* = β; loss = "rmspe" holds LS-SVR α. b Bias term. support_data Support-vector matrix (pruning) loss = "mape", full training matrix X loss = "rmspe". support_targets Support-vector targets loss = "mape"; NULL loss = "rmspe". n_train, n_sv, p_train Training counts. hyperparameters Named list (C, eps, gamma, ) NULL entries family apply. solver_meta Named list (backend, iters, converged, precondition_applied, spectral) describing solve. spectral slot populated symmetric MAPE fits (loss = "mape", sym != NULL) reports Algorithm 2 diagnostics (mu, lambda_min_hat, lambda_max_hat, branch_taken, n_power_iterations); NULL otherwise.

title: Fit a percentage-error SVR / LS-SVR model — psvr
text: arguments apply one family. loss = "mape", gamma precondition ignored (warning supplied non-NULL). loss = "rmspe", C, eps, solver, tol ignored (warning rule). Default values trigger warnings — user-supplied values , detected via missing().

title: Fit a percentage-error SVR / LS-SVR model — psvr
code: set.seed(1) X <- matrix(rnorm(40), 20, 2) y <- rlnorm(20) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  fit_mape  <- psvr(X, y, loss = "mape",  kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5) fit_rmspe <- psvr(X, y, loss = "rmspe", kernel = K, gamma = 100) fit_sym   <- psvr(X, y, loss = "rmspe", sym = +1L, kernel = K, gamma = 100)  predict(fit_mape,  X[1:3, , drop = FALSE]) #> [1] 0.8061082 0.7365284 0.8547480 predict(fit_rmspe, X[1:3, , drop = FALSE]) #> [1] 0.9189556 0.7369731 0.8524386 predict(fit_sym,   X[1:3, , drop = FALSE]) #> [1] 1.1583805 0.7439030 0.5084087

title: psvr 0.0.2.9002 (development)
text: .fit_mape_sym() (Model 2) now invokes Algorithm 2 (Theorem 2 arXiv:2605.01446 v3) via new .adaptive_spectral_shift() R/kernel-spectral.R. Two-pass shifted power iteration estimates λ_min(Ωs); numerically negative, adaptive μ·shift applied SMO Hessian provably PSD. Diagnostics (mu, lambda_min_hat, lambda_max_hat, branch_taken, n_power_iterations) surface psvr_fit$solver_meta$spectral symmetric MAPE fits; NULL Models 1, 3, 4. implementation deviates paper’s Algorithm 2 line 6 using spectral radius |Pass 1 Rayleigh| rather signed Rayleigh Pass 2 shift; rationale documented inline R/kernel-spectral.R flagged paper-side erratum F8. three Mercer kernels supported make_kernel() (RBF / linear / polynomial), Ωs always PSD Aronszajn closure / Schur product theorem, shifted branch unreachable production fits predictions bit-identical v0.0.2.9001 tolerance 1e-10 16 + 12 golden snapshots.

title: psvr 0.0.2.9001 (development)
text: SMO solver now reads kernel values via .make_kernel_accessor() wrapper (R/kernel-accessor.R), decoupling inner loop matrix representation. F2 implementation thin closure materialised Ω/Ωs; future phases (F3 spectral shift, F6 LRU cache, F7 block working set) swap alternative implementations without touching SMO loop fitters. user-visible change; predictions bit-identical v0.0.2.9000 tolerance 1e-10 16 + 12 golden snapshots.

title: psvr 0.0.2.9000
text: New unified entry point psvr(X, y, loss, sym, kernel, ...). Selects among four model families via loss = "mape" | "rmspe" sym = NULL | +1L | -1L. Returns single psvr_fit S3 object unified field schema (alpha, b, support_data, support_targets, n_train, n_sv, p_train, hyperparameters, solver_meta, plus loss, sym, kernel). New methods psvr_fit class: predict(), print(), coef(), summary(). summary method new — analogue four legacy classes.

title: psvr 0.0.2.9000
text: mape_svr(), mape_sym_svr(), rmspe_lssvr(), rmspe_sym_lssvr() soft-deprecated. continue work return legacy shape (psvr_mape, psvr_mape_sym, psvr_rmspe, psvr_rmspe_sym) predict/print/coef methods, emits .Deprecated("psvr"). Migrate replacing, example, mape_svr(X, y, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5) psvr(X, y, loss = "mape", kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5). Scheduled removal: v0.2.0 later.

title: psvr 0.0.2.9000
text: 12 parsnip fit wrappers (psvr_mape_rbf_fit() etc.) now tagged @keywords internal hidden pkgdown reference index. remain exported (parsnip’s resolver requires ) longer advertised user API; call [psvr()] direct fitting. 12 spec constructors (psvr_mape_rbf() etc.) remain public API unchanged. four model fitter bodies renamed internal helpers (.fit_mape(), .fit_mape_sym(), .fit_rmspe(), .fit_rmspe_sym()). deprecation wrappers delegate directly. DRY consolidation: shared validation, large-N warnings, predict dispatch, preconditioner resolution, kernel description live new internal-files R/utils-*.R. Model 2 (symmetric MAPE epsilon-SVR) now uses sym_kernel_matrix() build Ωs = ½(Ω + ·Ω*) (matching Model 4’s existing convention), diagonal jitter 0.5e-6 preserve bit-identicality pre-F1 path.

title: psvr 0.0.2 (2026-04-30)
text: precondition argument rmspe_lssvr() rmspe_sym_lssvr() now configurable via parsnip’s set_engine() six RMSPE spec functions (rbf/poly/linear, symmetric non-symmetric). Default "auto"; pass via set_engine("psvr", precondition = "always"), "never", numeric threshold. registered tunable parameter — configuration flag, hyperparameter.

title: psvr 0.0.1
text: Added optional preconditioner LS-SVR variants (rmspe_lssvr(), rmspe_sym_lssvr()) via precondition argument. Accepted values: “always”, “never”, “auto” (default; activates max(y)/min(y) > 10), numeric threshold. preconditioner mathematically exact change variable improves numerical conditioning large target dynamic ranges without changing predictions exact arithmetic. Returned model objects now include precondition_applied (logical) diagnostic transparency. print method displays field TRUE.

title: psvr 0.0.1
text: Updated @details LS-SVR fitters bordered-system formulation recovery formula α_k = α̃_k / y_k.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9005
text: cost_psvr_ls_data(y, n, width_log2) — data-driven cost range LS-SVR psvr models (m3, m4). Default upper bound scales log2(var(y) * N) + width_log2 (default width_log2 = 4), standard heuristic LS-SVR regularisation parameter Γ (Suykens et al. 2002, Least Squares Support Vector Machines, §3.1.3). Use instead cost_psvr() m3/m4 workflows; static [-2, 10] log2 range underestimates Γ benchmark datasets (Boston Housing optimum Γ ≈ 1.7 × 10⁴, vs. cost_psvr() upper bound 2^10 = 1024). psvr_option_add_cost_ls(wf_set, y, ...) — convenience wrapper applies cost_psvr_ls_data() every LS-SVR psvr workflow workflow set (whose wflow_id matches m3|m4), analogous psvr_option_add() rbf_sigma.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9004
text: sym_type now tunable parsnip argument psvr_mape_sym_rbf() psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(). Pass sym_type = tune() let CV select even (= 1) odd (= -1) symmetry automatically. Engine default = 1L removed symmetric RBF specs; symmetry type now controlled exclusively sym_type model argument.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9003
text: New function rbf_sigma_psvr_data(): combines sigma_heuristic() rbf_sigma_psvr() single call, returning dials parameter data-driven search range centred median pairwise distance. New function psvr_option_add(): applies option_add() every psvr workflow (whose wflow_id matches m1|m2|m3|m4) workflow set simultaneously, replacing rbf_sigma parameter data-driven one. Replaces four individual option_add() calls typical workflows. Symmetric model specs (psvr_mape_sym_*, psvr_rmspe_sym_*): even symmetry (= 1L) now default engine argument. Calling set_engine("psvr") without = 1L now produces result — action required existing code already passed = 1L. four core fitting functions now emit warning N > 2000 remind users O(n²) kernel matrix may expensive. y > 0 input check four core fitting functions now reports number offending values minimum, making easier diagnose data issues.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9002
text: New function margin_percentage(): dials parameter epsilon tube half-width MAPE models, expressed percentage units default range [1, 20]. Replaces absolute-unit dials::svm_margin() 6 MAPE specs. New function sigma_heuristic(): returns median pairwise Euclidean distance predictor matrix — standard data-driven starting point RBF kernel bandwidth (Schölkopf & Smola, 2002). New function rbf_sigma_psvr(): dials parameter RBF kernel bandwidth finalize hook auto-sets search range using sigma_heuristic() training data available. Replaces dials::rbf_sigma() RBF specs. New function cost_psvr(): dials parameter regularisation parameter range [-2, 10] log2 scale (approx. 0.25 1024), wider dials::cost() accommodate larger values typically needed LS-SVR models. Replaces dials::cost() 12 specs. rbf_sigma_psvr(): removed non-functional finalize hook. search range must set manually using sigma_heuristic() option_add(). See ?rbf_sigma_psvr recommended workflow.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9001
text: Expanded parsnip integration 4 12 model specs following tidymodels pattern used svm_rbf(), svm_poly(), svm_linear(): psvr_mape_rbf(), psvr_mape_poly(), psvr_mape_linear() — Model 1 specs. psvr_mape_sym_rbf(), psvr_mape_sym_poly(), psvr_mape_sym_linear() — Model 2 specs. psvr_rmspe_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_poly(), psvr_rmspe_linear() — Model 3 specs. psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_sym_poly(), psvr_rmspe_sym_linear() — Model 4 specs. Kernel parameters now tunable parsnip args mapped existing dials params: rbf_sigma → dials::rbf_sigma(), degree → dials::degree(), scale_factor → dials::scale_factor(). kernel closure built inside fit wrapper longer appears parsnip layer. old single-spec API (psvr_mape(), psvr_mape_sym(), psvr_rmspe(), psvr_rmspe_sym()) removed. Migrate replacing, example, psvr_rmspe(cost = tune()) |> set_engine("psvr", kernel = K) psvr_rmspe_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = 1) |> set_engine("psvr").

title: psvr 0.0.0.9001
text: vignettes pkgdown articles updated new spec-based API. 12 new smoke tests (fit + predict) specs tests/testthat/test-parsnip.R. pkgdown articles reorganised three named groups: Get Started, Case Studies, Technical Notes.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: Initial development release.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: mape_svr() — epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 1). Solves dual QP via osqp per-sample box constraints |βk| ≤ 100C/yₖ. mape_sym_svr() — symmetric epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 2). Enforces f(x) = ·f(-x) replacing kernel symmetric kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj). rmspe_lssvr() — LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 3). Solves (N+1)×(N+1) linear system directly via base::solve(). rmspe_sym_lssvr() — symmetric LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 4). linear system Model 3 symmetrized kernel matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + ·Ω*). four models derived unified percentage-error loss framework; see Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) mathematical proofs.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: make_kernel() — factory returning kernel closure type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy symmetry assumption (Assumption 3) required Models 2 4.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: Four parsnip model specifications use within tidymodels workflows: psvr_mape() — spec Model 1; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_mape_sym() — spec Model 2; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_rmspe() — spec Model 3; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). psvr_rmspe_sym() — spec Model 4; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). kernel (symmetric models) symmetry parameter engine arguments supplied via set_engine("psvr", kernel = ..., = ...). Hyperparameters map dials::cost() dials::svm_margin() compatibility tune_grid().

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: 84 unit tests covering four models: kernel correctness, QP/linear-system solutions, support vector selection, bias recovery, predict consistency, input validation.
